column1: # compare.tsv — the `cmp` three-way comparison, exhaustive over scalars.
# `a cmp b` yields -1 / 0 / 1 — a sorts before / with / after b, the
# same total order as lt / gt / sort (see eng/go/compare.go). The rows
# are the full cross product of a representative value of each scalar
# kind, a path value, and every scalar type literal.
#   1. Cross-family pairs (e.g. Integer vs String) settle by the
#      unified lattice Rank — no Comparer applies, so compareTypes
#      orders by Rank → depth → name → ID.
#   2. Same-family pairs settle through the family Comparer (Number,
#      String, Boolean, Atom, Path). Within a family:
#      a. Type literal vs concrete: type literal sorts FIRST.
#      b. Type literal vs type literal: by lattice Rank
#         (`Number cmp Integer → -1` because Number < Integer in Rank).
#      c. Concrete vs concrete: by value content (numeric magnitude,
#         lex, etc.). Cross-leaf numeric magnitude can tie
# The result is a strict total order over distinct lattice nodes; the
# only equivalence is cross-leaf numeric magnitude (1 == 1.0). See
# Cross-numeric magnitude — traditional arithmetic order survives the
# The Number Comparer (`numberCompareBehavior` on TNumber) is the
# lowest common ancestor for any Integer/Decimal pair; it projects
# both sides into float64 and orders by magnitude. The per-leaf Rank
# the LCA walk finds the Comparer first.

column3: # magnitude equality across Integer/Decimal collapses to 0

# Same length, same keyset, different values.

# Booleans < Numbers < Strings < Paths < Times    (Scalar 2·10¹⁰ band)
# Transitivity battery — for each chain a < b < c we assert a < c
# AND the conjunction of all three pairs. If any link breaks,
# transitivity fails and the test reports false.

(false lt 0) and (0 lt 1) and (1 lt 'a') and ('a' lt [1]) and ([1] lt {x:1})
(Any lt none) and (none lt Never) and (Any lt Never)
(Any lt none) and (none lt 5) and (Any lt 5)

# DepScalar ordering — canonical-form lex when paired with itself;

# User-defined types — every kind, type-literal and instance ordering.
# Each row declares its types inline (they live for the duration of
# the expression). The two interesting axes:
#   1. Same-kind ordering: user-type Rank shares one band per branch
#      band compareTypes tiebreaks by depth then lex name.
#   2. User-type vs kernel: kernel positional Rank (e.g. Object
#      4.1·10⁹) sits BELOW the user band (4.1·10¹⁰), so a user-
#      defined Object subtype sorts AFTER Object itself.
# Plus the within-family rule still applies: a user-type LITERAL
# sorts strictly below any concrete instance of that user type.
def Cat refine Object {color:String} def Dog refine Object {breed:String} Cat cmp Dog
def Cat refine Object {color:String} def Dog refine Object {breed:String} Dog cmp Cat
def Cat refine Object {color:String} def Dog refine Object {breed:String} Cat cmp Cat
def Cat refine Object {color:String} Cat cmp Object
def Cat refine Object {color:String} Object cmp Cat
def Cat refine Object {color:String} (make Cat {color:'orange'}) cmp Cat
def Cat refine Object {color:String} Cat cmp (make Cat {color:'orange'})
def Cat refine Object {color:String} (make Cat {color:'a'}) cmp (make Cat {color:'b'})
def Cat refine Object {color:String} (make Cat {color:'b'}) cmp (make Cat {color:'a'})
def Cat refine Object {color:String} (make Cat {color:'a'}) cmp (make Cat {color:'a'})
